package main

// Positive scenario for generic_process_execute_handle_service_name_without_domain_noun.
// Rule intent: Generic service names and methods that hide business intent instead
// of naming the actual use case with domain nouns.

// ProcessService is a generic name that reveals nothing about the domain.
// It should be named something like OrderFulfillmentService or PaymentProcessor.
type ProcessService struct{}

// Execute is a generic method name that hides what business operation is performed.
func (s *ProcessService) Execute(input map[string]interface{}) error {
	return nil
}

// Handle is equally generic and uninformative.
func (s *ProcessService) Handle(request interface{}) error {
	return nil
}

// Run adds another generic verb with no domain context.
func (s *ProcessService) Run(data []byte) error {
	return nil
}

// HandlerService is another generic name with no domain noun.
type HandlerService struct{}

func (s *HandlerService) Execute(payload string) error {
	return nil
}

func (s *HandlerService) Handle(ctx interface{}) error {
	return nil
}

// ManagerService follows the same anti-pattern.
type ManagerService struct{}

func (s *ManagerService) Process(items []string) error {
	return nil
}

func (s *ManagerService) Execute(task string) error {
	return nil
}

// Usage demonstrating the anti-pattern:
func example() {
	processSvc := &ProcessService{}
	processSvc.Execute(map[string]interface{}{"order_id": "123"})
	processSvc.Handle("some request")

	handlerSvc := &HandlerService{}
	handlerSvc.Execute("payload")
	handlerSvc.Handle(nil)
}
